claims.claim_text: Incident-style anecdote gives a 09:14 Monday bridge, a payments deployment stuck at half capacity, customer latency, and a ten-minute discovery timeline.
claims.anchor_text: A platform engineer joins an incident bridge at 09:14 on a Monday. The payments team has a deployment stuck at half capacity, customer-facing latency is climbing, and the cluster still has "nodes available" according to the dashboard. Ten minutes later, the real shape of the problem appears: the available nodes are the wrong shape. They have spare CPU, but not enough memory; they are in the wrong availability zone for a topology constraint; and the only autoscaling group allowed to grow is pinned to an instance family that is temporarily capacity-constrained.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A platform engineer joins an incident bridge for a production deployment that is stuck below its intended capacity. The dashboard still shows nodes available, but the available nodes are the wrong shape: they may have spare CPU but not enough memory, sit in the wrong availability zone for a topology constraint, or belong to an autoscaling group pinned to capacity the provider cannot currently supply.
claims.rationale: The anecdote is unsourced and contains fake-specific timeline and operational details, so the rewrite preserves the lesson while removing the unverifiable specifics.

claims.claim_text: Karpenter's most mature provider implementation is AWS.
claims.anchor_text: You do not need to be an AWS expert, but the examples in this module use EKS because Karpenter's most mature provider implementation is for AWS.
claims.suggested_rewrite: You do not need to be an AWS expert, but the examples in this module use EKS because this module focuses on Karpenter's AWS implementation.
claims.rationale: The superlative 'most mature' is not backed by a benchmark or governance source.

claims.claim_text: Karpenter watches unschedulable pods, evaluates their scheduling constraints, provisions nodes that meet those requirements, and removes nodes when no longer needed.
claims.rationale: The upstream README directly lists these Karpenter behaviors.

claims.claim_text: Cluster Autoscaler works with pre-configured node groups, while Karpenter supports auto-provisioning from constraints instead of fully configured homogeneous groups.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes Node Autoscaling page compares Cluster Autoscaler and Karpenter on this exact distinction.

claims.claim_text: Karpenter creates a NodeClaim after evaluating pending pod requirements and finding a compatible NodePool and NodeClass pair.
claims.rationale: The NodeClaims documentation describes NodeClaims as capacity requests created from pending pod requirements and matching NodePool/NodeClass constraints.

claims.claim_text: Autoscalers do not control actual pod scheduling; the scheduler remains the authority that places pods.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documentation states autoscalers predict scheduling outcomes but do not control actual scheduling.

claims.claim_text: If a pod's requirements do not overlap with NodePool requirements, Karpenter will not launch a node for that pod.
claims.rationale: The NodePools documentation explicitly says nodes are chosen using both pod and NodePool requirements and no node is launched when there is no overlap.

claims.claim_text: A NodePool can define constraints such as taints, zones, instance types, architectures, expiration, disruption policy, and resource limits.
claims.rationale: The NodePools documentation enumerates these NodePool configuration fields and behaviors.

claims.claim_text: An EC2NodeClass configures AWS-specific launch settings such as AMI family, role or instance profile, subnet and security group selector terms, block devices, metadata options, and tags.
claims.rationale: The EC2NodeClass documentation describes those AWS-specific fields.

claims.claim_text: NodeClaim status and describe output help debug launch, registration, initialization, and node readiness failures.
claims.rationale: The NodeClaims documentation says operators can inspect NodeClaims to see where node creation failed.

claims.claim_text: Karpenter disruption can remove or replace nodes for consolidation and drift, with disruption configured through NodePool fields and budgets.
claims.rationale: The disruption documentation describes consolidation, drift, consolidationPolicy, consolidateAfter, and NodePool disruption budgets.

claims.claim_text: Karpenter and node autoscaling decisions depend on pod resource requests rather than actual runtime usage.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes Node Autoscaling documentation states provisioning and consolidation consider resource requests, not actual usage.

claims.claim_text: PodDisruptionBudgets limit how many pods of a replicated application can be down during voluntary disruptions.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes disruptions page directly defines PodDisruptionBudget behavior for voluntary disruptions.

claims.claim_text: NodePool limits constrain the total size of the pool and prevent Karpenter from creating new instances once exceeded.
claims.rationale: The NodePools documentation states resource limits constrain pool size and prevent new instances after limits are exceeded.

claims.claim_text: EC2NodeClass metadataOptions control instance metadata service exposure, and httpTokens:[REDACTED_SECRET] enforces IMDSv2-style token use.
claims.rationale: The EC2NodeClass documentation describes metadataOptions and shows httpTokens:[REDACTED_SECRET] as the default.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes node labels include kubernetes.io/arch and topology.kubernetes.io/zone, and taints/tolerations affect whether pods can schedule onto nodes.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documents the standard node labels used in the examples; taint behavior is also documented under Kubernetes scheduling concepts.

claims.claim_text: Cloud provider capacity or incompatible provisioning configuration can leave pods pending even when autoscaling is enabled.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes Node Autoscaling documentation lists incompatible provisioning configuration and lack of cloud provider capacity as reasons provisioning may not make pods schedulable.

claims.claim_text: Amazon EC2 Spot capacity can be cheaper than On-Demand capacity and can be interrupted.
claims.rationale: AWS EC2 Spot best-practices documentation states Spot uses spare capacity at savings compared with On-Demand and can be interrupted.

claims.claim_text: Karpenter supports native interruption handling for Spot and other involuntary interruption events when the interruption queue is configured.
claims.rationale: The Amazon EKS Karpenter best-practices page describes Karpenter interruption handling and the interruption queue setting.

claims.claim_text: Karpenter consolidation can delete empty nodes, remove nodes whose workloads can run elsewhere, or replace nodes with lower-priced variants.
claims.rationale: The disruption documentation describes Karpenter consolidation deletion and replacement mechanisms.

claims.claim_text: Karpenter has evolved its APIs over time, including changes around NodePool, NodeClaim, and provider resources in the v1 transition.
claims.rationale: The AWS Karpenter 1.0 announcement discusses API evolution from v1beta1 to v1 and related resource conversion behavior.

claims.claim_text: AWS created and open-sourced Karpenter in 2021 to address infrastructure selection, provisioning, and scaling limitations.
claims.rationale: The CNCF Karpenter v1.0 blog directly states AWS created and open-sourced Karpenter in 2021 and describes its purpose.

claims.claim_text: Allowing more EC2 instance types can improve Karpenter's ability to optimize Spot capacity availability and cost; narrow requirements can leave pods pending.
claims.rationale: The EKS Karpenter best-practices page advises avoiding overly constrained instance types, especially for Spot, and explains pending pods when alternatives are unavailable.

claims.claim_text: KEDA provides event-driven autoscaling for Kubernetes workloads and works with Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler integration.
claims.rationale: The KEDA README describes KEDA as a Kubernetes-based event-driven autoscaling component integrated with the HPA.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Node Autoscaling
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains node provisioning, consolidation, Cluster Autoscaler, and Karpenter in Kubernetes terms.

further_reading.title: Amazon EKS Best Practices: Karpenter
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers Karpenter usage patterns on EKS, Spot guidance, interruption handling, and request-based scheduling behavior.

further_reading.title: Karpenter NodePools
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines NodePool requirements, taints, disruption settings, limits, and compatibility rules.

further_reading.title: Karpenter NodeClaims
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains how NodeClaims represent capacity requests and help debug provisioning failures.

further_reading.title: Amazon EC2 Spot Best Practices
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides authoritative background for Spot pricing, interruption risk, and workload suitability.

notes: Code blocks, ASCII diagrams, quiz items, and exercise command steps were not treated as citation targets except where surrounding prose made load-bearing vendor, security, pricing, or standard-behavior claims.
